title: Justice May Be Wounded, but It Will Surely Rise and Triumph!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked derived reading copy in which clear OCR character, punctuation, numeral, legal-term, and word-break errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: In the disproportionate-assets case against Jayalalithaa, the Karnataka Special Court sentenced her on 27 September 2014 to four years' imprisonment and a fine of Rs 100 crore. She was imprisoned and lost the Chief Minister's office. She went to the Supreme Court seeking bail. The Chief Justice of India released her on bail, but his order drew criticism even then.

It was alleged that Chief Justice Dattu acted contrary to legal convention: even in an ordinary case, the opposing side would be heard before bail was granted, but neither Karnataka nor Tamil Nadu's Directorate of Vigilance and Anti-Corruption was heard. Though the Supreme Court itself had required reasons when granting bail in corruption appeals, the Chief Justice set aside the Karnataka High Court's forty-page refusal in four lines without stating reasons. Though the four convicted persons sought only bail, he also ordered the appeal expedited. With hundreds of cases pending, critics called this “super” special treatment irregular and a warning to the public. The People's Rights Protection Centre petitioned the President with the signatures of one thousand lawyers seeking action against Chief Justice Dattu.

Before the bail plea was heard, social activist Traffic Ramasamy complained that Fali Nariman's appearance for Jayalalithaa violated Bar Council norms because Nariman's son was a Supreme Court judge. He also petitioned Chief Justice Dattu to withdraw, citing a corruption complaint concerning the case.
After obtaining bail, Jayalalithaa remained at home and appealed. Following a three-month hearing, Justice Kumaraswamy acquitted all four accused on 11 May 2015.

Special Public Prosecutor B.V. Acharya said: “The entire appeal proceedings were conducted without making Karnataka State a Party, though it was a necessary legal requirement. Counsel for the accused [were] allowed to make oral arguments for nearly two months, but no prosecutor authorised by Karnataka was present during such arguments. Thereby the principles of natural justice were denied to the prosecution during the appeal proceedings.” He added: “In my fifty-eight years as a lawyer, I have never seen such a judgment. It is one-sided. It does not state the charges against the accused. The Supreme Court gave the prosecution only one day and permitted no oral argument, directing only written submissions.”
Although Justice Kumaraswamy acquitted them, the country saw many contradictions between observations he made during the hearing and his final judgment. Let me recall a few.

Discussing Sudhakaran's wedding, the judge asked: “Was Sudhakaran an ordinary AIADMK cadre or Jayalalithaa's son that so many crores were spent on such a magnificent wedding? You have argued for twenty-eight days and accuse the Special Court judge of an erroneous decision, yet have not established its defect. What can I do? I may have to confirm the Special Court judgment. You have no evidence denying assets worth Rs 66 crore. Produce evidence or I shall appoint an auditor, examine the accounts myself and deliver judgment without even Bhavani Singh's argument.” Yet his judgment said Jayalalithaa spent only a few lakhs on the wedding. Is that not a contradiction?

On 16 February 2015, Jayalalithaa's counsel P. Kumar called it a false case filed for political revenge. The judge replied: “You say ‘false case’ hundreds of times, but how can oral assertion be accepted without evidence showing the complaint untrue? The prosecution examined 259 witnesses and filed 2,341 documents. The defence examined ninety-nine witnesses and filed only 385 documents, with little of substance to defeat the prosecution.”

When Kumar said Jayalalithaa accepted a salary of only one rupee as Chief Minister, the judge said: “Even at a salary of one rupee, a Chief Minister is a public servant. All are equal before law. The Vigilance Department has established that Jayalalithaa possessed income of Rs 66 crore. You have offered no evidence denying it.”

When Sasikala's lawyer Manishankar said Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi were convicted as abettors without basic evidence, the judge asked how the Special Court could have convicted people who had not conspired, how police could register and conduct an eighteen-year case without evidence, and how the Special Court could decide it without examination. He told counsel to prove errors with evidence instead of wasting time, adding that not even twenty per cent of the case had been argued with evidence.

On another occasion he said the DVAC had proved 82 to 92 per cent of its charges, while the defence had argued only 30 to 35 per cent: “Thirty-five marks may pass you at school, but in court you must score more than the other side. They have scored sixty-five marks more, so they pass.” Why, then, did his judgment fail the side with sixty-five marks and pass the side with thirty-five?

He also said judgment could not rest on oral argument but only evidence, documents and witnesses; that the allegation was that Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi accumulated property using Jayalalithaa's income as Chief Minister; and that the defence had filed nothing proving the allegation false.

When counsel Sudhandhiram concluded on the twentieth, the judge said: “The hearing ran for thirty-one days. You alleged a false DVAC case, but have finished without producing the evidence I requested.” He said the trial judgment had tied 150 knots establishing guilt and the appeal had to untie each with evidence, yet the defence had not attempted to untie any.

He further said the Special Court judge had studied 65,000 pages, examined 252 witnesses, heard both sides and written a 1,000-page judgment only after full analysis. The defence had not proved its accusations wrong, so he might have to affirm Judge Cunha's judgment. This very judge, after expressing all these views openly, acquitted Jayalalithaa and the others.

AIADMK cadres celebrated, and their party newspaper carried more than one hundred pages of congratulatory advertisements. We have no bitterness about that. We do not desire that Jayalalithaa be sent to prison or suffer punishment. But everyone expects equality before law, the rule of law and the distinctive dignity of justice to be upheld.

In the TANSI case, the Supreme Court itself observed that public-sector land was sold to companies in which Jayalalithaa was a partner while she was Chief Minister; that approval was immediately supplied by the government machinery; and that, whatever the property's valuation or her profit, officials showed excessive interest in completing the sale at the price she desired. It asked whether there could be one law for ordinary officials and another for a Chief Minister, whether the code of conduct was merely an ornament for a museum, and noted that Jayalalithaa went so far as to deny her own signature on the deed, though accepted by her auditor and officials.

As in TANSI, she has now been acquitted here. But is Justice Kumaraswamy's judgment proper? Has any judgment in judicial history contained such confusion and errors? Will lawyers and judges accept it?

A schoolchild's addition error can be excused. But can a major judgment resting upon an easily detectable, enormous addition error be accepted as lawful? What did the judgment make eight plus seven? It effectively made it thirty-five. Jayalalithaa's side took ten bank loans to start companies. After listing each loan, the judge totalled them as Rs 24 crore. But whoever adds the ten obtains only Rs 10 crore. Treating the mistaken Rs 24 crore as income, he concluded that Jayalalithaa had only Rs 2 crore in excess assets, eight per cent of income, and acquitted her.

Using the actual total of Rs 10 crore changes the disproportion from eight per cent to seventy-six per cent, making her liable to punishment. Through this erroneous calculation a major conviction was reversed. After Judge Cunha spent months carefully examining documents, witnesses and arguments, should a High Court judge have made such errors while overturning his judgment?

Lawyers and political observers point to other errors. A senior woman lawyer asks whether, since many listed loans had been repaid with interest, the judge should not also have calculated the income used for those repayments.

Bhavani Singh was supposed to prosecute the appeal, but a separate case seeking his removal reached the Supreme Court because, it was alleged, he did not properly oppose the accused. The fact that Jayalalithaa's own side fought up to the Supreme Court to retain him speaks for itself.

A three-judge Supreme Court Bench ultimately ordered that Bhavani Singh be removed, that his submissions not be accepted, and that Karnataka appoint another prosecutor. Justice Dipak Misra wrote: “We are absolutely sure that the learned Single Judge, as the appellate Judge, shall keep in mind the real functioning of an appellate court. The appellate court has a duty to make a complete and comprehensive appreciation of all vital features of the case. The evidence brought on record in entirety has to be scrutinized with care and caution. It is the duty of the Judge to see that justice is appropriately administered for that is the paramount consideration of a Judge. The said responsibility cannot be abdicated or abandoned or ostracized, even remotely, solely because there might not have been proper assistance by the counsel appearing for the parties. The appellate Court is required to weigh the materials, ascribe concrete reasons and the filament of reasoning must logically flow from the requisite analysis of the material on record. The approach cannot be cryptic. It cannot be perverse. The duty of the Judge is to consider the evidence objectively and dispassionately. The reasonings in appeal are to be well deliberated. They are to be resolutely expressed. An objective judgment of the evidence reflects the greatness of mind, sans passion and sans prejudice. The reflective attitude of the Judge must be demonstrable from the judgment itself. A judge must avoid all kind of weakness and vacillation. That is the sole test. That is the litmus test. This being the position of a Judge, which is more elevated as the appellate Judge, we are of the considered opinion that there is no justification for rehearing of the appeal as the matter has been heard at length and reserved for verdict.”

Why did the Supreme Court give such important advice in advance? Did it anticipate that something could go wrong? Was that advice followed? A shopkeeper's addition error costs a few rupees. What remedy exists for such an absurd addition error in a judgment affecting a major case?

The judgment's page 853 is headed “Gifts as income,” and page 854 treats foreign remittances as income, though a CBI prosecution concerning them was pending in the Supreme Court. Conduct rules prohibit Ministers from receiving gifts. Accepting gifts and foreign remittances as lawful income breeds corruption.

Justice Kumaraswamy wrote that Jayalalithaa claimed gifts worth Rs 2.15 crore and foreign money, and assessed Rs 1.50 crore as income from gifts received around her birthday and foster son's wedding. One fact was carefully omitted: she received them not merely as a party leader but while Chief Minister. Receiving them itself violated the conduct rules. How could they be counted as income? With a CBI case pending, how was it proper to treat those gifts as income?

Senior Supreme Court lawyer Lily Thomas, whose case led to legislators convicted of corruption losing office, asked why Jayalalithaa alone received an expedited decision while appeals including Lalu Prasad Yadav's remained pending. Whatever its basis, she said, the resulting situation was worrying.

Kumaraswamy acquitted Jayalalithaa principally because her assets had been disclosed to and accepted by the Income Tax Department. But he did not consider that the returns were filed only after the disproportionate-assets case began, or that an income-tax decision does not bind a court.

It was said that Jayalalithaa would return as Chief Minister on the seventeenth, after which delayed Assembly grant debates and completed but unopened projects would move. But after newspapers exposed errors in the judgment, she hesitated to emerge and the legislators' meeting did not occur. Reports now say they will meet on the twenty-second and she will take office that day.

In 2000, a Chennai Special Court sentenced Jayalalithaa in two TANSI land cases. Her four nominations in the 2001 Assembly election were rejected because she was disqualified. Yet after the AIADMK won a majority, its legislators hurriedly chose her leader and, with Governor Fathima Beevi's assistance, she became Chief Minister on 14 May 2001. On 21 September, the Supreme Court ruled that appointment invalid.

In the Pleasant Stay Hotel case, a Special Court sentenced her to one year's rigorous imprisonment, but she was acquitted on appeal. While Chief Minister from 1991 to 1996, she received foreign drafts worth 300,000 dollars, said she did not know the sender, yet deposited them. A case was filed, but she was later discharged. Cases also concerned alleged corruption in free colour televisions, losses from substandard coal, the return of SPIC shares held by TIDCO, and other matters. Though convicted in some, she obtained acquittals on appeal.
Likewise, though this case will inevitably reach the Supreme Court, she now intends to become Chief Minister without concern for that.

Most Tamil Nadu parties have called for a Supreme Court appeal. Suspicions are also voiced about the BJP government's conduct. The Prime Minister telephoned congratulations immediately after the acquittal; Union Minister Sushma Swaraj congratulated her; Pon Radhakrishnan congratulated her and said she should become Chief Minister; and Finance Minister Arun Jaitley visited her home even before judgment. Why has the BJP, which criticised the AIADMK government, changed? Tamil Nadu Congress leader E.V.K.S. Elangovan has alleged a connection between the judgment and the BJP government. Subramanian Swamy, who first filed the case, is now in the BJP. When that party's leaders congratulate the person acquitted in his case, must we not ask, “Bhimsingh, what new confusion is this?”
It was suggested Justice Kumaraswamy might recognise the grave error and amend his judgment, but Karnataka's Chief Justice has barred alterations.

With the statistical foundation on which the acquittal rests shattered, Tamil Nadu awaits the outcome. The media have described the uncertainty created in the administration. At a time when people fear justice itself has been harmed, everyone who believes in democracy and the Constitution bears responsibility for protecting it, especially Karnataka, which has the first right of appeal. Attempts are reportedly being made to divert it too. To leave no room for such criticism and correct the error, Karnataka must recognise the importance of justice and immediately announce an appeal to the Supreme Court. I insist upon it once again.
With affection,
M.K.
